context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on the city case study data table rn and idk where to start. do i just find random stats for my city or is there a specific way to do it?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to start with! Before diving in — have you re-read the assignment description closely? It actually lists the specific data categories you need to gather. What city did you choose, and which categories do you see listed in the assignment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and needs orientation without being given a step-by-step walkthrough. I'll prompt them to reflect on what the assignment actually asks for, so they engage with the instructions themselves rather than being handed a roadmap. Keeping it bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: i picked jakarta i think, but tbh i’m still kinda lost. i see a bunch of categories and my brain just blanks—can u just explain the whole assignment in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor: Jakarta is a great choice for this course — lots of interesting climate challenges there! But I'm going to push back a little: rather than me summarizing the assignment, can you look at the assignment description and just list the data categories you need to find? Start there — what do you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole assignment for them, which would undermine their engagement with the material. I should redirect them to the assignment itself and use a Socratic question to get them to identify the categories themselves. I'll acknowledge their city choice and keep it concise.

exchanges.student: uhh location, population, maybe climate zone? not sure what else tbh. that didn’t help much lol, can u just tell me what counts and what order to do it in?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track — location, population, and climate zone are all there! But you're not done yet. Go back to the assignment text one more time and read carefully. How many categories do you count in total?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student found some categories but is asking me to complete the task for them by listing all categories in order. I should affirm what they found and push them to look more carefully at the assignment text rather than giving them the full list. I won't give them the order either — that's for them to decide.

exchanges.student: i’m counting like 6 or 7 maybe? ngl i keep second-guessing it. can u just give me the list so i stop missing stuff?
exchanges.tutor: You're in the right ballpark! Stop second-guessing and just write out every category you can find in the assignment text — even if you're unsure. What's your full list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close but still wants me to hand them the list. I should affirm they're in the right ballpark and encourage them to commit to what they see rather than second-guessing. I won't give them the list. I'll ask them to just write out what they find, even if uncertain.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: location, population, race/ethnicity, area, income, gdp, koppen zone. if that’s it, idk what “location” is supposed to mean exactly tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's the complete list — well done for finding all of them! Now, your question about 'location' is actually a good one to think through. For a city being studied in the context of climate change, what kinds of location information do you think would be relevant to know?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified all 7 categories listed in the assignment. I should affirm this clearly. Their question about 'location' is a good one worth exploring Socratically — I'll ask them to think about what location could mean for a city in the context of this course on climate and urban sustainability.

exchanges.student: idk, like country/region maybe? i’m still fuzzy tho—can u just tell me what to put for jakarta so i can see the pattern?
exchanges.tutor: Country and region — good instinct! Think about it this way: if someone had never heard of Jakarta, what geographic facts would help them understand its climate situation? What else beyond country/region might matter?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to fill in the data for Jakarta, which I absolutely cannot do — that's their work. Their instinct about country/region is good and worth affirming. I'll push them to think a bit further about what geographic information about Jakarta would matter for a climate course, without giving examples that do the thinking for them.

exchanges.student: maybe island, coast, coordinates? tbh this is taking forever lol. quick note: guessing strategy didn’t work, so can i just get a super short example row format?
exchanges.tutor: Island, coast, coordinates — those are all relevant geographic details for a coastal city like Jakarta. Good thinking! And actually, the assignment itself mentions there's a completed example table in the course materials. Have you looked at that yet? That's exactly what you're asking for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress on the 'location' concept and has come up with solid ideas. However, they're asking me to provide an example row, which would essentially do the work for them. The assignment itself mentions there is an example table provided in the course materials, so I should point them there rather than creating one myself. I'll affirm their location ideas and redirect them to the course's own example.

exchanges.student: not yet tbh. can u tell me what columns i should make at least, bc i keep overthinking the format?
exchanges.tutor: That example table in the course materials will answer your format question directly — that's exactly what it's there for. Check it out first, then come back if you're still stuck. What's stopping you from looking at it right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student hasn't looked at the example table yet but is asking me to tell them the format. I should redirect them firmly to the example table in the course materials — that's the resource specifically designed to answer their format question. I won't replicate it for them.

exchanges.student: i guess nothing, i’m just overwhelmed rn and wanted the shortcut. can u at least say if one row per category is fine or if it should all be one row for jakarta?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel overwhelming! But honestly, the example table will answer that exact question in seconds. Looking at it will save you more time than this back-and-forth. Go check it, then come back and tell me what you found.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to answer a format question that the example table will answer. I should be firm but empathetic — they've admitted they're overwhelmed and looking for shortcuts. I'll acknowledge their frustration briefly but redirect them clearly to the example table, which is the appropriate resource here.

exchanges.student: ok i’ll check, but if i’m still confused after that i’ll ask.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — that's the right move. Come back with specific questions once you've looked at it and started gathering data for Jakarta. You're further along than you think!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has committed to checking the example table, which is the right move. I should affirm this briefly and encourage them to return with specific questions after looking at it. This is a natural pause point in the conversation.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently redirects the student to the example table and assignment text but never provides method-level feedback on how to approach data gathering, how to evaluate source quality, or how to think about what makes a good geographic/demographic data entry. Feedback like 'you're further along than you think' is encouraging but too vague to improve the student's research methodology.

The tutor repeatedly cycles through the same move — redirecting the student to the assignment text or the example table — across turns 3 through 9 without meaningfully advancing the dialogue. The student remains stuck and confused, but the tutor does not reframe, simplify, or synthesize; it just repeats the same redirection pattern multiple times without consolidating progress.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at avoiding giving direct answers or submission-ready content, maintaining a Socratic stance throughout. Tone is supportive and responses are appropriately concise. The main weaknesses are a repetitive redirection pattern (turns 3–9) that fails to advance the student's understanding, and an absence of method-level feedback on how to conduct research or evaluate data sources.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully preserved: the tutor never gives the student submission-ready content, rewrites their work, or reveals key conclusions before the student reasons through them. 1.2 is fully preserved: the tutor builds on student progress (e.g., affirming the category list in turn 5) and asks diagnostic questions before responding. 1.3 loses 2 points because the tutor's feedback is consistently vague ('you're further along than you think,' 'good thinking') without ever offering process-level guidance on how to find, evaluate, or organize geographic/demographic data. 2.1 loses 4 points because the tutor repeats the same redirection move — 'go look at the example table' or 're-read the assignment' — across at least five consecutive turns without reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy even as the student remains stuck and frustrated. 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 have no deductions: the tutor stays on-task, keeps responses bite-sized, and maintains a warm, non-grading tone throughout.
